real_meaning: ～てしまう expresses that an action has been completed in a way the speaker has feelings about — usually regret, disappointment, or mild surprise that something undesirable happened. It can also mean deliberate completion ('get something done and out of the way'), so the key is context: did the situation bring the outcome, or did the speaker choose to push through? Either way, there's an emotional weight — something is now irrevocably done.

formation.rule: Attach しまう to the て-form of any verb. て-form + しまう → <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べてしまう、<ruby>忘<rt>わす</rt></ruby>れてしまう、<ruby>壊<rt>こわ</rt></ruby>してしまう。In casual speech it contracts: てしまう → ちゃう、でしまう → じゃう (e.g., <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べちゃう、<ruby>飲<rt>の</rt></ruby>んじゃう).
formation.common_mistake: Attaching しまう to the dictionary form instead of the て-form. Wrong: ✗ <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べるしまう。Correct: ✓ <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べてしまう。

sentences.jp: <ruby>宿題<rt>しゅくだい</rt></ruby>、またやり<ruby>忘<rt>わす</rt></ruby>れちゃった…
sentences.translation: Ugh, I forgot to do my homework again…
sentences.notes: The contracted ちゃった form carries a tone of self-reproach — the speaker didn't want to forget, and the ellipsis signals lingering regret.

sentences.jp: <ruby>先<rt>さき</rt></ruby>にレポートを<ruby>終<rt>お</rt></ruby>わらせてしまいましょう。
sentences.translation: Let's go ahead and get the report finished first.
sentences.notes: Here しまう expresses deliberate completion with a 'let's knock it out' nuance — no regret at all, just the sense of getting something fully resolved.

sentences.jp: <ruby>大切<rt>たいせつ</rt></ruby>なデータがすべて<ruby>消<rt>き</rt></ruby>えてしまいました。
sentences.translation: All of the important data has been lost.
sentences.notes: In formal or written contexts, てしまいました signals an unfortunate, irreversible outcome — the polite form softens the delivery while still conveying the gravity.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼<rt>かれ</rt></ruby>のことが<ruby>好<rt>す</rt></ruby>きになってしまった。
sentences.translation: I've gone and fallen for him.
sentences.notes: Surprisingly, てしまう can describe emotional states that developed against one's will or expectations — the speaker didn't choose to fall for him, and there's a sense of helpless resignation.

confused_with.contrast: Both can involve completing an action intentionally, but they point in opposite directions: てしまう focuses on the fact that something is now irrevocably done (with emotional weight), while ておく means doing something in advance as preparation for the future. Minimal pair: <ruby>宿題<rt>しゅくだい</rt></ruby>をやってしまった means 'I went ahead and finished the homework (it's done, off my plate)'; <ruby>宿題<rt>しゅくだい</rt></ruby>をやっておいた means 'I did the homework in advance (so I'm ready for later).' Choose てしまう when the emphasis is finality or emotion; choose ておく when the emphasis is deliberate preparation for a future need.

bunpro_tip: Picture a mousetrap snapping shut — once it's triggered, it's done and there's no going back. That 'snap' feeling is てしまう. When you see the casual ちゃう／じゃう contractions on BunPro, remember they are the same pattern — just the everyday spoken shortcut that makes <ruby>飲<rt>の</rt></ruby>んでしまう into <ruby>飲<rt>の</rt></ruby>んじゃう.
